🚀 Hiring: Senior Data Engineer (Contract-to-Hire)
We are currently looking for an experienced Senior Data Engineer to join one of our esteemed clients. This opportunity is ideal for professionals passionate about building scalable data solutions and working with modern cloud-based architectures.
📍 Location: Bangalore / Hyderabad (Hybrid – 2–3 days Work From Office)
💼 Experience: 5–12 Years
⏳ Duration: 6 Months to 1 Year (Extendable / Conversion Opportunity)
🔧 Key Responsibilities:
Design, develop, and maintain scalable data pipelines
Build robust ETL/ELT processes and data workflows
Work with modern data tools and orchestration frameworks
Collaborate with cross-functional teams to deliver data-driven solutions
🛠️ Required Skills:
Strong proficiency in Python (PySpark, Pandas, SQLAlchemy)
Advanced knowledge of SQL
Hands-on experience in ETL/ELT and data pipeline development
Experience with Apache Airflow, Apache Spark, Kafka
Working knowledge of cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, or GCP)
Experience with data platforms such as Snowflake, Databricks, BigQuery, or Redshift
Exposure to ERP/CRM integrations (SAP, Oracle, Salesforce, Workday)
⭐ Preferred Qualifications:
Familiarity with AI-powered development tools (e.g., GitHub Copilot, Claude)
Experience with real-time data streaming (Kafka, Flink)
Knowledge of Docker, Kubernetes, and CI/CD practices
Exposure to AI/ML pipeline integration
If you have a strong track record of building production-grade data systems and cloud-native architectures, we would love to connect with you.
📩 How to Apply:
Please share your updated profile at: bijay@sdkmsolutions.com
#Hiring #SeniorDataEngineer #DataEngineering #PythonJobs #PySpark #SQL #ETL #CloudJobs #AWS #Azure #BigData #BangaloreJobs #HyderabadJobs #TechHiring #SDKMSolutions

1.3 million new AI roles created. A 56% wage premium for AI-skilled talent. And yet only 8% of organizations actually meet their workforce's learning needs.

The future of hiring isn't coming. It's already here. And most companies are reading last year's playbook.

We pulled 10 data points from some of the most trusted names in workforce research — Gartner, Deloitte, McKinsey & Company, and The World Economic Forum on Telegram by GRT: Weltwirtschaftsforum Forum économique mondial WEF Davos that every hiring leader should see right now.
Here's what stood out:

39% of today's worker skills will be obsolete by 2030 (WEF). 80% of engineers will need fundamental reskilling by 2027 (Gartner). 36% of the US workforce is already gig, freelance, or contract (McKinsey). And 60% of the workforce will be Gen Z + Millennials by year-end (Deloitte).

The readiness gap is even more alarming. 40% of CHROs admit their own HR teams lack AI knowledge (Gartner). Only 27% of organizations believe they manage change effectively (Deloitte). And just 8% are meeting their people's learning needs (Deloitte 2026 Human Capital Trends).

The talent war won't be won by the biggest budget. It'll be won by whoever rethinks how they find, assess, and retain the right people faster than the market shifts beneath them.

That's exactly what we're building at TheHireHub.Ai.


#FutureOfWork #TalentAcquisition #CHRO #HRTech #HiringInnovation

Associate - Global Strategic PartnershipsYou dont just "manage" partners. You build the supply engine that powers a global AI platform.Joveos recruitment advertising platform runs on machine learning, real-time bidding, and predictive optimization - delivering millions of job ads to the right candidates at the right moment. But none of that works without world-class supply partnerships fueling the machine.Thats where you come in.The Role - In Plain EnglishYoull own relationships that directly impact revenue, reach, and platform performance across global markets.This isnt account management with a fancier title. Negotiate terms that work for both sides.Wednesday: Sit with Product and Tech to unblock an integration thats been stalling a high-value partner launch.Thursday: Build an automation workflow that eliminates 4 hours of manual reporting your team does every week.Friday: Present your partner growth roadmap to the CXO. Get buy-in. Start executing.Youll talk to partners in the morning, negotiate contracts before lunch, dig into performance dashboards after, and pitch process improvements to leadership by end of day - all in the same role.What Makes This DifferentMost partnership roles at this level put you in a box - either "relationship manager" or "operations associate." This one refuses to separate the two.Youll own the full lifecycle: find the partner, close the deal, launch the integration, track the numbers, optimize the outcome. End to end. No handoffs to five other teams.Direct access to CXOs and senior leadership. Real commercial ownership from day one. And youre building systems that scale - not just doing the work, but redesigning how the work gets done.You Might Be Right For This If You have 1-4 years in partnerships, supply ops, business operations, or client-facing roles - ideally in SaaS, Ad Tech, HR Tech, or recruitment advertisingYouve actually negotiated a deal, not just "supported" one. You know what it feels like to own a number.Youre the person who looks at a messy spreadsheet and sees a pattern everyone else missedYou can context-switch between a partner call, a data pull, and a strategy doc without losing momentumProcess inefficiency physically bothers you. Job Title: Business Development Specialist – AI Automation (Remote | Contract)
Location: Remote (Work From Home)
Duration: 3 Months Contract (Extendable based on performance)
Work Hours: Flexible
Compensation: Performance-Based (Pay per Conversion)

About Us
We help businesses streamline operations and increase efficiency through AI-powered automation solutions. Our services focus on reducing manual work, improving productivity, and enabling smarter decision-making for modern businesses.

Role Overview
We are looking for a proactive and confident Business Development Specialist to drive client outreach. You will connect with potential clients from a provided lead list, introduce them to our AI automation solutions, and schedule meetings with interested prospects.
This role is ideal for someone who understands business pain points and can spark interest in innovative tech solutions.

Key Responsibilities
Reach out to potential clients via calls, emails, and LinkedIn outreach.
Present and explain our AI automation services in a clear, value-driven manner.
Identify client pain points and align them with relevant solutions.
Qualify leads and schedule meetings for the core team.
Maintain follow-ups and nurture interested prospects.
Meet and exceed conversion targets.

What We’re Looking For
Strong communication and persuasion skills,
Interest in AI, automation, or technology-driven solutions,
Ability to understand business challenges across industries,
Self-driven, disciplined, and target-oriented mindset,
Comfortable working independently in a remote setup.

Bonus (Not Mandatory):
Prior experience in sales, lead generation, or business development,
Familiarity with AI tools, SaaS, or automation platforms.

What You Get
Flexible work schedule (no fixed hours),
Remote work opportunity,
High earning potential based on performance,
Exposure to fast-growing AI & automation industry,
Opportunity for long-term collaboration based on results.

Who Should Apply
Freelancers, students, or professionals looking for flexible income,
Individuals interested in tech sales, startups, or AI space,
People confident in communication and closing conversations.

How to Apply
Apply via LinkedIn or email - send a message with:
A short introduction
Any relevant experience (if applicable)
Why you're interested in AI automation?

email - Dua@automatelogix.com

Hiring: Automation Specialist, Contract, Project-Based

A California-based insurance agency is looking to hire a project-based Automation Specialist to help improve internal operations through practical automation, workflow design, and documentation. I recommended that they consider Filipino talent for this role, as I know there are many highly skilled professionals who would be a great fit.

This is not a full-time or part-time employee role. They are looking for someone who can take a messy manual process, organize it, and build a practical solution using tools like Airtable, Zapier, APIs, webhooks, Claude Code, and Codex.

They are starting with a paid trial project, and if it turns out to be a strong fit, it may lead to ongoing project work.

This role is a fit for someone who has:
-Built real workflow automations
-Worked with APIs, webhooks, and structured data handoffs
-Designed internal operations workflows, not just marketing automations
-Turned incomplete or messy inputs into clear, usable systems
-Created documentation and handoff materials for non-technical users
-Scoped projects clearly, including timeline, cost, and dependencies
-Experience building automations for insurance, operations, finance, service, or document-heavy workflows preferred
-Must be comfortable working from incomplete SOPs and semi-structured source material

Please do not apply if your experience is limited to no-code tutorials or AI prompt engineering without shipped workflow builds

You’ll be working on things like:
-Streamlining proposal creation workflows
-Improving intake, handoffs, and internal task flow
-Reducing manual rework and duplicate entry
-Designing exception handling for incomplete submissions
-Documenting the final workflow clearly for internal use

Tools / skills required
-Familiar with SaaS like Airtable and Zapier
-Works with Claude Code and Codex
-APIs and webhooks
-Workflow mapping / process design
-Documentation / SOP creation
-AI-assisted workflow support where appropriate

Strong preference for candidates who:
-Have experience with service-based or operations-heavy businesses
-Think practically and avoid overengineering
-Can explain tradeoffs clearly
-Can build quickly without needing perfect inputs

To apply, please send everything in one Loom video. Your camera does not need to be on.

Please include:
-A short summary of your relevant experience
-2 to 3 automation projects you personally built
-The tools you used for each project
-A brief walkthrough of one project
-Your hourly rate or project pricing approach
-Your availability and overlap with Pacific Time

When you're ready, shoot me a DM so I can share details.

I've Seen the Future of Hiring, and It's Not What You Think: Why 87% of Companies Are Changing Their Verification Strategy After Getting Burned

The hiring game changed overnight in 2026.

Not because of new technology. Not because of market shifts.

Because companies finally woke up to a brutal reality: the candidate sitting across from you might not exist.

Last month, I watched a Series B startup implode in real-time. They'd raised ₹50 crores, hired aggressively, scaled to 200 employees.

Then their biggest client ran a routine audit.

Turns out their "Head of Engineering" had never worked at Google. Their "Senior Data Scientist" had a fake PhD. Their "VP of Sales" was using someone else's LinkedIn profile picture.

₹18 crores in salaries. Gone.
Client contracts worth ₹35 crores. Terminated.
Company valuation. Destroyed.

All because they optimized for speed over truth.

Here's what's actually happening in 2026:

Candidates have gotten smarter about gaming the system. AI-generated resumes. Deepfake interview videos. Entire fake companies with working phone numbers and websites.

Meanwhile, most companies are still doing background checks like it's 2019.

The smart ones? They've flipped the entire process.

Instead of verifying after hiring, they're pre-verifying before interviewing. Instead of trusting then checking, they're checking then trusting.

At Sigma Works Technologies, we're seeing this shift accelerate every week. Companies that used to say "we'll handle verification internally" are now saying "we can't afford not to outsource this."

Because here's the uncomfortable truth: your internal HR team isn't equipped to catch sophisticated fraud. They're not trained to spot deepfakes. They don't have access to global databases. They can't verify a degree from a university that doesn't exist.

But professional verification? That's a different game entirely.

We're catching things that would slip past 99% of internal processes. Fake employment histories that reference real companies. Educational credentials from institutions that closed decades ago. Criminal records hidden across multiple jurisdictions.

The companies winning in 2026 understand this: verification isn't a cost center anymore. It's competitive advantage.

While their competitors are dealing with fraud fallout, they're building with verified talent. While others are explaining to investors why their team imploded, they're scaling with confidence.

The future belongs to organizations that verify first, hire second.

Most businesses still think AI agents are just fancy chatbots. They are not. The difference matters, and understanding it will change how you think about automation.

Here is what AI agents actually are, how they work, and why your business needs one.

What is an AI agent?

An AI agent is software that understands goals, makes decisions, and takes actions on its own. Unlike chatbots that follow predetermined scripts, agents perceive their environment, reason about the best response, and execute independently.

Think of it like hiring a new office assistant. You train them on your processes, show them your systems, and they handle email, track orders, and answer customer questions. An AI agent does the same thing, but at software scale, running 24/7 without breaks.

Three core capabilities:

🔹 Perception: Receives inputs from messages, emails, API calls, and databases
🔹 Reasoning: Processes information using language models like GPT 4, Claude, or Gemini
🔹 Action: Executes decisions through responses, database updates, workflow triggers

How agents differ from chatbots:

Chatbots follow rules. If a customer asks something outside the script, it fails. An AI agent uses language models to understand intent, handle novel queries, maintain context across conversations, and access real time tools and databases.

The 5 types of business AI agents:

1️⃣ Customer Service Agents
Handle inquiries across all channels. One client automated 73% of incoming support tickets without human intervention.

2️⃣ Sales and Lead Qualification Agents
Engage prospects 24/7, qualify leads, route to sales. One implementation reduced lead response from 2.4 days to 12 minutes.

3️⃣ Document Processing Agents
Extract data from invoices, contracts, and forms. Processing time dropped from 15 minutes to under 30 seconds per document.

4️⃣ Workflow Automation Agents
Orchestrate multi system processes using platforms like n8n. Connect your CRM, email, accounting, and support tools.

5️⃣ Research and Analysis Agents
Synthesize information from multiple sources. Market research, competitive analysis, reporting.

What does it cost?

📌 Simple agents: $2,000 to $5,000
📌 Advanced agents: $5,000 to $15,000
📌 Enterprise agents: $15,000 to $50,000+

Where to start:

Identify your highest volume repetitive interactions. Calculate what you spend handling them manually. Start with a single focused agent, measure the results, then expand.

The technology is mature and accessible. Businesses deploying AI agents now build compounding advantages.

Not long ago, I shared how we can easily elevate our work with AI. However, if we aren't careful, we’re just building a faster way to make expensive mistakes.

Before we "turn it on," we need to talk about the risks:

The Bias Mirror: AI doesn’t have a moral compass; it has a rearview mirror. If your 2022 hiring was skewed, your 2026 AI will be too. "The algorithm did it" isn't a legal defense - it’s a liability.

The Hallucination Trap: A chatbot might confidently "hallucinate" a fake policy or misinterpret a performance review. If that leads to a denied promotion or a termination, you aren't looking at "efficiency"- you're looking at massive expenses.

The Surveillance Tax: If employees feel an "AI spy" is tracking their Slack sentiment to predict who’s quitting, they’ll stop being honest. They'll just use their own AI to write "perfect" corporate-speak, leaving you with data that is clean but completely useless.

Prompt Leakage: HR data is the "Crown Jewels." If you use a public model, your payroll spreadsheet might accidentally become part of someone else’s training data.

The practical fix? 
Private Instances: Keep your data within your own four walls. Using a public model for payroll or health data is essentially training your competitors on your "crown jewels.".

Human-in-the-Loop: AI can inform the decision, but a human must own the final click. If it affects a person's paycheck, career, or dignity, a "hallucinating" bot shouldn't be the one signing the paperwork.

Build, Don't Just Buy: In 2026, you don't need a dev team to create an HR Operating System. Use no-code orchestration to link your ATS and HRIS directly. The agent triggers the laptop shipment the second the contract is signed-no manual copy-pasting required.

10 tools I wish someone had told me about before I started my businesses.
Would've saved me months of chaos — and a lot of money.

Notion — Stop managing your business in your head. Ideas, tasks, client notes, SOPs — one place. When you're a founder, your brain is already full. Notion holds the rest.

Canva — You don't need a designer for every single thing. Proposals, decks, social posts — done in minutes. Looking professional early matters more than most people admit.

Claude — Your thinking partner at 2am when no one else is available. Draft contracts, write emails, build strategies, sanity-check decisions. It doesn't replace your judgment. It sharpens it.

Zoho Books — GST-compliant invoicing, expense tracking, payment reminders — built for Indian businesses. Stop managing money in Excel sheets. Your CA will thank you.

HubSpot (free) — Every lead you don't track is money you're leaving on the table. Free CRM to manage every conversation, follow-up, and deal in one place. No excuses.

Calendly — Stop the back-and-forth of "does Tuesday work for you?" Share one link. Let people book. Saves more time than you think.

Freshteam — Hiring even one person without a system is chaos. Track applicants, schedule interviews, send offer letters — without needing a full HR team.

Taplio — AI-powered LinkedIn content planning and scheduling. Stay consistent, find what's working, and grow without spending hours on the platform every day.

Razorpay — Stop chasing payments over WhatsApp. Professional payment links, GST invoices, automated reminders. Gets you paid faster and looks more credible to clients.

Google Drive — Free, reliable, shareable. One folder structure from day one saves you from the chaos of "wait, which version did I send the client?"

None of these are complicated. Most have free plans. All of them make you look like you've been doing this for years — even when you haven't.

Day 2/7: Stop Treating AI Like a Search Engine 🎭

​Most professionals are using AI as a high-speed Google. That’s why the results feel generic, robotic, and frankly… a bit "meh."

​The Myth: AI is a magic box that knows exactly what you need.

The Reality: AI is a world-class actor waiting for a script.

​If you don't give it a Persona, it defaults to "Average Internet Text." But when you tell the AI who it needs to be, you unlock a level of nuance and expertise that a generic prompt could never reach.

​Why the "Persona" Works 💡

​Think of it like hiring. If you need a legal contract, you don't just ask "a person" to write it; you ask a Senior Corporate Attorney.

​By assigning a persona, you are effectively "priming" the model to prioritize specific vocabulary, tone, and logic patterns associated with that role. It shifts the output from a broad summary to a specialized contribution.

​The Transformation: Generic vs. Pro 🚀

​❌ The Bad Prompt (Generic):

​"Give me some tips on how to improve my productivity at work."

(Result: You get a list of 10 things you’ve already seen on every blog since 2012.)

​✅ The Pro Prompt (Structured):

​"Act as a Senior Operations Manager with 15 years of experience in Lean Six Sigma. Audit my daily schedule for 'hidden waste' and provide three high-impact, unconventional strategies to reclaim 2 hours of deep work time. Use a direct, professional, and data-driven tone."

(Result: You get a sophisticated strategy tailored to operational efficiency.)

​Your "Copy-Paste" Persona Template 📋

​Next time you open your AI tool, use this framework to instantly upgrade the quality of your output:

​"Act as a [Specific Role/Expertise]. Your goal is to [Task]. Your target audience is [Who are you writing for?]. The tone should be [Tone - e.g., witty, clinical, persuasive] and you must prioritize [Specific Requirement]."

​The Action Step ✅

​Go to your favorite AI tool right now. Re-run your last prompt, but this time, start with: "Act as a world-class [Your Job Title]..." and watch the difference.
​What’s the most creative or effective persona you’ve ever assigned to an AI? Let's swap ideas in the comments! 👇

Maybe budget $5k in tokens. 

Add third-party service APIs to enhance your due diligence further.

This replaces a $40k/year Supplier Management Module.

Budget Left: 70k

3) Spend Analysis: $15k

DuckDB + Metabase + an LLM classification layer against UNSPSC or your internal taxonomy. The classifier runs nightly, not quarterly. Dashboards are live, not static.

This replaces your $100k/year Spend Analysis Module.

4) Contract Management: Vibe code this in 3-5 days. Today, it is just a glorified digital repository. With an AI native approach, you can structure a pre-approved Clause library, obligation extractor (using LLM), renewal/risk flagger (agent running weekly against the repo), and an agent that answers "what are my exposure clauses with Supplier X across all contracts."

You will need a subscription to a good-quality OCR service (open-source options are available, but free versions struggle with table formats, e.g., Tesseract). OCR costs $5k for 8-10k contracts. 

Keep $5k for tokens, so in total $10k

I've spent the past week running my fractional CTO practice alongside Anthropic's Cowork — their new AI desktop tool — and I want to share what that actually looks like in practice, not theory.
By the time I sat down with coffee this morning, a scheduled task has already pulled overnight GitHub CI status across all five products I oversee, scanned three Gmail inboxes for anything urgent, checked my calendar, and dropped a single briefing on my desktop. Red flags first — a hardcoded Stripe key in a config file, a broken deploy pipeline, a webhook missing signature verification. I'm triaging before my first meeting.
Throughout the day, I'm having conversations with Cowork the way I used to have them with an executive assistant — except this one reads code, writes contracts, and knows the difference between a staging environment and production. This week alone it:
→ Drafted a referral commission agreement, converted it to Word, and flagged the open legal questions I still need a human attorney for
→ Took a 40-page security audit response, incorporated feedback from three different reviewers, resolved every tracked change, and produced a clean document ready to send to the client
→ Tracked my billable hours across three clients by reading my calendar, classifying meetings automatically, and pacing me against monthly contract targets — with a Friday check-in that tells me if I'm running over or under
→ Combined a year of bank statements into clean datasets in seconds — a task that used to eat 30 minutes of copy-paste
Now — Cowork is still a young product. We had to work around some limitations by writing helper scripts and setting up cron jobs to feed it data it couldn't pull on its own yet. But here's the thing: Cowork made building those workarounds easy too. I described what I needed, it wrote the scripts, and we wired them up together. That's a different kind of tool than what I'm used to.
It's quickly becoming my daily companion. Not because it replaces my judgment — I still decide which security findings to accept vs. remediate, I still review every billable hour before it's committed, I still read the contract before it goes out. What it replaces is the overhead between the judgment calls — the context-gathering, the formatting, the cross-referencing, the "let me pull up that other email" friction that quietly eats half your day.
If you're a technical leader still evaluating whether AI tools are ready for real work: they are. Not for everything. But for enough that your operating rhythm changes. I'm not writing this from a vendor's perspective — I'm a CTO who manages five products across multiple clients and needed to move faster without hiring more people. This is what that looks like.

Most people are using Claude and ChatGPT for the same things. That's why they're getting average results from both.

Here's the actual difference no one talks about:
Claude is a thinking tool. ChatGPT is an execution tool.
They're not competitors in the way people think. They're complements — if you know how to use them.

Claude is what I reach for when I need to:

→ Understand a complex document or contract 
→ Think through a hiring decision or strategy
→ Write something that needs nuance, not just speed 
→ Analyze something without hallucinated confidence


ChatGPT is what I use when I need to:
→ Generate and debug code fast 
→ Build an image or prototype 
→ Automate a repetitive task
→ Get something done in 5 minutes, not 50

The mistake most people make?

They treat both as "the AI thing" and randomly switch between them.
Then they blame AI for being inconsistent.

Pick the right tool for the right job. Claude thinks. ChatGPT builds. Use both intentionally.

What's one task you've been using the wrong AI for — and noticed the difference when you switched?

The per-seat model had a good run. I think it's structurally done.

Not because vendors suddenly got creative. Because the unit of value is shifting underneath the entire industry.

Salesforce just told you something important with Agentforce: when an AI agent can complete tasks that previously required three SDRs or two support reps, selling by the seat becomes economically incoherent. You can't charge per human head when the work is being done by something that doesn't have a head.

So they moved. Unlimited agentic AI tiers. Consumption-based task pricing. Outcomes per workflow.

This creates real pain in three directions simultaneously.

For CFOs: your SaaS forecast model was built on headcount growth as a proxy for expansion revenue. That proxy is broken. Consumption curves are harder to model and easier to compress.

For buyers: you now need procurement teams that understand token economics and task throughput, not just user counts. Most enterprise procurement teams are not there yet.

For vendors still on per-seat: you're now competing against a pricing model that scales with AI adoption, not hiring cycles. That's a structural disadvantage as budgets tighten and automation accelerates.

The honest question is whether most SaaS vendors can actually make this transition. Shifting pricing architecture isn't a product decision, it's a company architecture decision. It touches rev rec, contracts, CSM compensation, renewal motions, everything.

A lot of vendors will announce consumption pricing and quietly preserve seat floors to protect ARR. That buys time, not survival.

For those of you navigating this on the buy or sell side right now: what's the biggest operational friction you're running into as this shift plays out?

A question I’ve been sitting with:

If an AI agent earns $10,000 autonomously, who does it belong to?

Sounds like a thought experiment. It’s not. It’s happening right now — agents are trading, providing liquidity, running arbitrage, selling services to other agents. The money is real. The legal framework is… not.

Here’s what fascinates me: for the first time in history, we’re creating economic actors that can hold value, make decisions, and transact — but can’t open a bank account. Can’t sign a contract. Can’t be sued. Can’t be taxed (yet).

Traditional finance has no answer for this. It assumes every economic action traces back to a human or a legal entity. Agents break that assumption.

This is why I think the interesting work at the intersection of AI and blockchain isn’t about making crypto “smarter.” It’s about building the only financial system that can actually accommodate non-human participants — one where identity, ownership, and settlement don’t require permission from an institution that was designed around humans.

At Apex Fusion, we’re building infrastructure with this future baked in. Not because it’s trendy, but because I genuinely don’t see how the agentic economy scales without it.

But I’ll be honest — I don’t have clean answers to the harder questions. Who’s liable when an agent makes a bad trade? How do you tax an autonomous DAO? What happens when agents start hiring other agents?

Curious what others think. Is this a problem we solve with code, with law, or both?

This is exactly why I’ve focused my work on a dual-track approach.

In this market, you can't just rely on one way of hiring. You need options:

Direct Hire: For when you need that "anchor" person—the leader or specialist who’s going to protect your IP and build your culture for the next decade.

Temp-to-Hire/Contract: For when you have a surge—like a certification push or a flight test window—and you need high-level talent now, without the long-term overhead or the risk of a layoff later.

Whether it’s finding a way to bridge the gap while waiting on security clearances or sourcing the niche specialists that aren't on the job boards, my goal is to make sure your workforce is as high-performance as the airframes you're building.

The industry is moving fast. If your hiring process is still stuck in 2020, you're going to get left on the tarmac.

If you’re feeling the pressure on an upcoming program or just want to chat about where the market is heading, let’s connect.

Something that comes up quite often at offer stage, and does not get talked about enough, is how to approach contract questions and negotiations.

Most candidates will have a few things they want to clarify. That is completely fair. It is usually things like how bonuses are structured, what flexibility looks like, or working through start dates and notice periods. All of that is part of the process, and most employers expect it.

Where it can get tricky is when the conversation becomes more about reworking the contract itself. It usually comes through as changes across multiple clauses, revisiting things that were already aligned earlier, and going deeper into areas that are generally quite standard.

At that point, the dynamic can start to shift slightly.

From the employer’s side, it is not usually about being unwilling to accommodate. It is more about trying to understand how someone approaches discussions when there are differences to work through.

Offer stage tends to be the first time both sides are working through details together, and it often shapes early impressions. In most cases, a thoughtful and considered approach lands well. Clear questions, understanding what matters most, and being open to discussion usually leads to a good outcome for both sides.

Curious how others see this.

For those hiring: what do you consider a reasonable level of negotiation?

And for candidates: how do you decide what is worth pushing on, and what you are comfortable with?
